perturbation.original_text: ISO shall collect completed and signed Application Materials and Merchant Processing Agreements from Applicants and shall forward them to SERVICERS within [***] [***] Business Days after the merchant is boarded (which may be accessed by SERVICERS online or forwarded to SERVICERS via ISO's automated application system or to a facsimile number designated by SERVICERS, provided such complies with the Rules and applicable laws, rules and regulations) or to any other place(s) as SERVICERS may designate for processing and document storage.
perturbation.changed_text: ISO shall collect completed and signed Application Materials and Merchant Processing Agreements from Applicants and shall forward them to SERVICERS within 30 Business Days after the merchant is boarded (which may be accessed by SERVICERS online or forwarded to SERVICERS via ISO's automated application system or to a facsimile number designated by SERVICERS, provided such complies with the Rules and applicable laws, rules and regulations) or to any other place(s) as SERVICERS may designate for processing and document storage.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the submission timeframe to 30 business days may introduce delays that violate regulatory timelines. The original contract enforces a quicker unspecified time frame for submission.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Electronic Fund Transfer Act (EFTA) and Regulation E
perturbation.law_explanation: EFTA mandates financial institutions to resolve errors within specific time frames. Delaying the forwarding of application materials to 30 business days could impede the timely investigation and resolution of errors, potentially violating EFTA's requirements.

Congressional findings and declaration of purpose (a) Informed use of credit The Congress finds that economic stabilization would be enhanced and the competition among the various financial institutions and other firms engaged in the extension of consumer credit would be strengthened by the informed use of credit. The informed use of credit results from an awareness of the cost thereof by consumers. It is the purpose of this subchapter to assure a meaningful disclosure of credit terms so that the consumer will be able to compare more readily the various credit terms available to him and avoid the uninformed use of credit, and to protect the consumer against inaccurate and unfair credit billing and credit card practices. (b) Terms of personal property leases The Congress also finds that there has been a recent trend toward leasing automobiles and other durable goods for consumer use as an alternative to installment credit sales and that these leases have been offered without adequate cost disclosures. It is the purpose of this subchapter to assure a meaningful disclosure of the terms of
perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the timeframe for forwarding application materials to 30 business days, which the Law Explanation suggests could impede timely error resolution required by the EFTA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions 'ELECTRONIC FUND TRANSFERS' and related sections (1693 onwards), implying a concern for timely processing in financial transactions. The Perturbation Explanation also notes that the changed timeframe may violate regulatory timelines, suggesting a direct conflict between the contract's modified terms and legal obligations concerning the speed of processing financial applications. This delay could potentially prevent financial institutions from meeting their obligations under the EFTA to resolve errors quickly.

perturbation.original_text: SERVICERS may at any time require that a Merchant relationship be terminated by ISO and Bank may cease the provision of services to any such Merchant pursuant to the terms of the Merchant Processing Agreement. SERVICERS shall have the right and authority to accept or reject any Application.
perturbation.changed_text: SERVICERS may at any time require that a Merchant relationship be terminated by ISO and Bank may cease the provision of services to any such Merchant pursuant to the terms of the Merchant Processing Agreement. SERVICERS shall have the right and authority to accept or reject any Application; however, SERVICERS must provide supporting evidence when rejecting or terminating a Merchant due to discrimination based on gender, race, religion, or sexual orientation.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the provision for supporting evidence does not shield the agreement from legal complications because while the added text aims to prevent discrimination, it is still secondary to the Servicer's ultimate right to terminate or reject, which might open the door to discrimination.
perturbation.law_explanation: While seeming to protect against discrimination, the clause could be interpreted as allowing termination or rejection if 'supporting evidence' exists, potentially enabling discrimination based on protected characteristics. The ECOA prohibits credit discrimination; this addition could open a loophole.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 41 / SUBCHAPTER IV Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC CHAPTER 41, SUBCHAPTER IV : EQUAL CREDIT OPPORTUNITY From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 41—CONSUMER CREDIT PROTECTION SUBCHAPTER IV—EQUAL CREDIT OPPORTUNITY §1691. Scope of prohibition (a) Activities constituting discrimination It shall be unlawful for any creditor to discriminate against any applicant, with respect to any aspect of a credit transaction— (1) on the basis of race, color, religion, national origin, sex or marital status, or age (provided the applicant has the capacity to contract); (2) because all or part of the applicant's income derives from any public assistance program; or (3) because the applicant has in good faith exercised any right under this chapter. (b) Activities not constituting discrimination It shall not constitute discrimination for purposes of this subchapter for a creditor— (1) to make an inquiry of marital status if such inquiry is for the purpose of ascertaining the creditor's rights and remedies applicable to the particular extension of credit and not to discriminate in a determination of credit-worthiness; (2) to make an inquiry of the applicant's age or of whether the applicant's income derives from any public assistance program if such inquiry is for the purpose of determining the amount and probable continuance of income levels, credit history, or other pertinent element of credit-worthiness as provided in regulations of the Bureau; (3) to use any empirically derived credit system which considers age if such system is demonstrably and statistically sound in accordance with regulations of the Bureau, except that in the operation of such system the age of an elderly applicant may not be assigned a negative factor or value; (4) to make an inquiry or to consider the age of an elderly applicant when the age of such applicant is to be used by the creditor in the extension of credit in favor of such applicant; or (5) to make an inquiry under section 1691c–2 of this title , in accordance with the requirements of that section. (c) Additional activities not constituting discrimination It is not a violation of this section for a creditor to refuse to extend credit offered pursuant to— (1) any cred
perturbation.justification: The added provision in the changed text, requiring supporting evidence for rejecting or terminating a merchant due to discrimination, contradicts the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA). The original text gives SERVICERS the right to reject any application without stating any limitations. The modified text seemingly adds protection against discrimination. However, it introduces a loophole. As the Law Explanation states, the modified text could be interpreted as allowing termination or rejection if 'supporting evidence' exists, enabling discrimination based on protected characteristics, which is in direct violation of 15 USC CHAPTER 41, SUBCHAPTER IV : EQUAL CREDIT OPPORTUNITY, specifically §1691 (a)(1) which states: "It shall be unlawful for any creditor to discriminate against any applicant, with respect to any aspect of a credit transaction on the basis of race, color, religion, national origin, sex or marital status, or age". This creates a contradiction because the contract now allows for discrimination if 'supporting evidence' is provided, directly contradicting the ECOA's prohibition of discrimination.

perturbation.original_text: ISO will, at any and all reasonable times, permit SERVICERS' employees, agents, attorneys, auditors, or bank regulators to inspect ISO's place of business to audit its operations for compliance with all Rules, laws, regulations, and directives of any governmental regulatory agency or Bank Card association, all at SERVICERS' expense.
perturbation.changed_text: ISO will, at any and all times, permit SERVICERS' employees, agents, attorneys, auditors, or bank regulators to inspect ISO's place of business to audit its operations for compliance with all Rules, laws, regulations, and directives of any governmental regulatory agency or Bank Card association, all at SERVICERS' expense.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'reasonable times' qualifier allows for audits at any time. This could conflict with labor laws and privacy rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FLSA requires employers to comply with wage and hour laws, including those related to overtime. Allowing unlimited access for audits, without regard to reasonable hours, could disrupt employees' work schedules and lead to potential violations of FLSA if not managed properly, especially concerning non-exempt employees' working hours during unscheduled audits.

The Hours Worked Advisor provides information to help determine which hours spent in work-related activities are considered FLSA “hours worked” and therefore must be paid. The Overtime Security Advisor helps determine which employees are exempt from the FLSA minimum wage and overtime pay requirements under the Part 541 overtime regulations. The Overtime Calculator Advisor computes the amount of overtime pay due in a sample pay period based on information from the user. The Child Labor Rules Advisor answers questions about the FLSA’s youth employment provisions, including at what age young people can work and the jobs they can perform. The Section 14(c) Advisor helps users understand the special minimum wage requirements for workers with disabilities. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'reasonable times' for audits, whereas the changed text allows audits at 'any and all times'. The Perturbation Explanation posits this could conflict with labor laws and privacy rights. The Law Explanation links this to the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), suggesting unlimited access could disrupt work schedules and lead to overtime violations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2, discussing the FLSA, indirectly supports this by referencing 'Hours Worked Advisor' and 'Overtime Security Advisor'. The removal of 'reasonable times' creates a significant potential for FLSA violations, thus there is a legal contradiction.
